prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response:
“Tienes Razón” by Helado Negro.  
The whole song is just one soft, looping confession — “tienes razón, me estoy muriendo” (“you’re right, I’m dying”) — sung in a voice so calm it feels like the moment after crying when your breath finally evens out. The percussion is a heartbeat made of fingers on a tabletop, the chords sway like a hammock; nothing rises, nothing breaks, it just stays there and lets the truth sit on your chest. It moves me because it refuses to dramatize grief: it presents decay as a quiet, almost courteous process, and in doing so makes survival feel possible — not heroic, just continuous, like keeping the metronome going while the room slowly empties.
